The sound of alerts stacking on top of alerts. Marisol has her own terminal, quieter, in a side room she has claimed for observation. She is graphing the coordination patterns. The chart does not look like a market. It looks like a crowd.
MARISOL (to her terminal, to herself, to the data): "This is not a trade. This is a demonstration. The question is what they're demonstrating against — and whether they know the answer themselves."
#216 · 2021 The Dose and the Distance
Simón Reyes, Wei Lin, Conrad Powers
By mid-2021, wealthy nations have vaccinated the majority of their populations while most low-income countries have received less than 2% of global vaccine supply; China distributes its Sinovac vaccine to over 100 countries through its 'vaccine diplomacy' program.
Simón has spent his existence watching the asymmetry between what he produces and what he receives. The vaccine rollout is asymmetry made mortal — the difference between access and no access is now measured in lives, openly, on dashboards anyone can read. Wei Lin arrives in his markets with doses. Not charity — Wei Lin does nothing that isn't also strategy — but the doses are real and the timing is noted and Simón notes it with the particular attention of someone who has been keeping score for decades and has just seen the scoreboard shift.
A vaccination site in an emerging market capital — a converted stadium or exhibition hall. Long lines outside in heat. Inside, the controlled choreography of the clinic: numbered stations, gloved hands, the small moment of the needle, the fifteen-minute observation wait. Simón moves through it not as a recipient but as an observer — he is watching what it means for people to receive this thing and then watching whose flag is on the box it came in.
SIMÓN (to Wei Lin, who has arrived with shipments, whose government flag is on the packaging): "You understand this will not be forgotten." WEI LIN (arranging the logistics, efficient): "I understand it very well." SIMÓN: "I'm not sure that was a compliment." WEI LIN: "I know."
#217 · 2021 Transitory
Aurora Lux, Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega
U.S. inflation reaches 7% by December 2021 — the highest rate in forty years — as supply chain disruptions, stimulus spending, and pent-up demand converge; Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell initially characterizes the inflation as 'transitory.'
Aurora has watched inflation arrive before. She knows what it looks like at the beginning — the specific way it enters quietly, the way the official voices call it temporary because they need it to be temporary, the way the price of eggs moves before the price of labor, the way ordinary people feel it in the body before it appears in the report. She says nothing publicly. She doesn't need to. Her price has already begun to move. She is the oldest instrument in this system and she is already measuring what the official instruments have decided to call a phase.
A grocery store, fluorescent and ordinary. The prices on the shelf tags. The slight wrongness of them — not dramatic, not crisis, just off by enough that a person who has been shopping here for years pauses and reads the tag twice. Aurora moves through the aisle unhurried. She has been in this aisle in 1921 and 1946 and 1973 and 1980. She knows how it develops. She picks up nothing. She is not here to shop. She is here to observe.
AURORA (to Marisol, who has found her in the aisle): "They're calling it temporary." MARISOL: "You think it's not." AURORA (setting the item back on the shelf): "I think 'temporary' is a word people use when they need more time to be right. I've heard it before. The thing that comes after is usually less temporary."
#218 · 2021 The Attention Economy
Marisol Vega, Conrad Powers
In October 2021, a Facebook whistleblower presents internal research to Congress showing the company knew its platforms caused harm to teenagers; the company rebrands as Meta days later.
Marisol monitors the flows of value through the financial system, but she has always understood that money moves where attention moves first. What she is watching now is an economy built entirely on the capture of attention — not as metaphor, but as the literal product: the seconds of a human mind directed at a screen, sold to the highest bidder. She is not alarmed by the harm to teenagers specifically; she is alarmed by the structure, which is a financial system she did not design and does not control.
A Congressional hearing room — the particular theater of it, the raised dais, the cameras, the witness table where a young woman sits with printed documents. Marisol watches from the gallery. Conrad is there too. They are both watching someone testify about an exchange that runs on something other than money and learning that the exchange is larger than they thought.
MARISOL (to Conrad, gallery seats, leaning close): "This platform processes more human decisions per day than your entire banking system." CONRAD (watching the testimony): "I know." MARISOL: "Does that concern you?" CONRAD: "It concerns me that I don't know whether to be concerned or not. Which is a new feeling."
#219 · 2021 The Stranger at $69,000
Aurora Lux, Marisol Vega, Conrad Powers
Bitcoin reaches an all-time high of approximately $69,000 per coin on November 10, 2021, bringing its total market capitalization above $1.2 trillion.
Aurora has known about the stranger since 2009. She has watched him grow from a curiosity to a speculation to a mania to a correction to a mania again. At $69,000 he is impossible to ignore — not because she fears him, but because she is being asked, constantly now, to compare herself to him, and she finds the comparison neither flattering nor insulting but simply wrong. She is five thousand years old. He is twelve. She has been through every crisis the world has produced. He has been through one cycle and called it a thesis. She does not dismiss him. She studies him.
A trading screen somewhere displaying two lines: Bitcoin's parabolic ascent and gold's steadier, more modest climb. Someone in the room has drawn a circle around the intersection of the lines — the moment when the two assets moved in opposite directions — and written a question mark. Aurora is in the room. The Bitcoin line is very tall. Her line is respectable, deliberate, ancient. She puts her finger on her own line. She does not touch his.
AURORA (to Marisol, studying both lines): "People keep asking me if I'm afraid of him." MARISOL: "What do you tell them?" AURORA: "I tell them I don't fear things I don't understand yet. I study them." A beat. "He reminds me of every young thing that has ever been sure it was new."
#220 · 2022 February 24th
Conrad Powers, Zane Mercer, Sofia Valente, Marisol Vega, Aurora Lux
Russia launches a full-scale invasion of Ukraine on February 24, 2022, the largest military assault in Europe since World War II.
Conrad has been in wars before. He was born in one. But this war lands differently — not because of the scale, which history has seen larger, but because of the specificity of the threat it poses to the post-1945 order he has spent eighty years building. Zane is immediately activated: Russia is an energy power and energy is the first weapon deployed. Sofia's heating costs will triple. Her dependence on Russian gas — which she understood in the abstract — becomes visceral overnight. Marisol is at her instruments before the first missile lands.
A newsroom, 4 a.m., the broadcast that goes live before the light changes in Kyiv. The television image — grainy, real-time, the specific look of something being recorded on a phone by someone who is afraid. Every character is somewhere watching a screen. Conrad in Washington. Sofia in Brussels. Zane is on the screen — he is the commodity being weaponized; he has no choice about it but he is aware of every use he is put to.
MARISOL (to Conrad, on a call, minutes after the invasion begins): "He's going to use Zane as the first weapon. Then you'll use the financial system." CONRAD: "That's the plan." MARISOL: "The plan will work. I'm not worried about the plan. I'm worried about who is watching you execute it and what they're writing down."
#221 · 2022 The Weapon
Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega, Wei Lin, Simón Reyes, Hiro Nakamura, Sofia Valente, Aurora Lux
Following the invasion of Ukraine, the United States and its allies freeze approximately $300 billion in Russian central bank reserves held in Western financial institutions and cut major Russian banks from the SWIFT international payments network.
This is the moment that will be studied. Every country in the world — every country that holds reserves in Conrad's system, every country that routes payments through SWIFT, every country that has ever been in a room with Conrad and wondered whether the invitation to the table was permanent — every country notes this moment and does a calculation it has never had to do before. Conrad is not wrong to use the tool. He is right that Russia violated international law. But Marisol has spent her existence studying second-order effects, and the second-order effect of this decision is that the weapon has been seen.
Multiple rooms, simultaneous. The Treasury Department press briefing where the numbers are announced. The Bank of Russia, where officials discover they cannot access their own reserves. A diplomatic call between Wei Lin and Simón that does not appear in any official record. Hiro on a call with Sofia, both processing what this means for their own reserves. And Aurora, alone in her room with a glass of something amber, reading the announcement with the particular attention she gives to documents that will be cited for decades.
MARISOL (to the room, to anyone who will hear it, to the record): "The moment a weapon is used, every potential target recalibrates. That is not opinion. That is physics." A beat. "He has just told every country on earth what happens when he decides you are on the wrong side. Some of them will decide to stay on his side. Some of them will decide to find a different side. And some of them will decide to build a door he cannot lock."
#222 · 2022 Heating Bill
Sofia Valente, Zane Mercer, Conrad Powers
European natural gas prices surge by over 400% in 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine and subsequent manipulation of gas supplies to Europe, triggering an energy crisis across the continent.
Sofia has been dependent on Russian energy for decades and she has always known it in the way you know something you have chosen not to examine directly. The war makes it impossible not to examine. Zane is in her building whether she invited him or not — he is in her heating system, her electricity grid, her industrial supply chain — and the price he is now charging her is political as much as economic. She has survived worse than this. But she has never before had to explain to her citizens why their heating bill is a geopolitical variable.
A European household in winter — not a palace, not a government office, an ordinary apartment. The radiator that is not as warm as it was last year. The thermostat set lower. A utility bill on the kitchen table, opened, placed face-down. Sofia does not live in households but she moves through them; she is made of these places. She and Zane are in the same room in a way that has never been comfortable and is now actively painful.
SOFIA (to Zane, in the cold kitchen of the ordinary apartment, the bill face-down between them): "You don't choose your uses. I understand that." ZANE (unapologetic, present): "No." SOFIA: "But you could at least not look satisfied." ZANE (a long pause): "I don't choose that either."
#223 · 2022 The Most Aggressive Since Volcker
Conrad Powers, Aurora Lux, Hiro Nakamura, Simón Reyes
The Federal Reserve raises interest rates eleven times between March 2022 and July 2023, lifting the federal funds rate from near zero to over 5% — the most aggressive monetary tightening cycle since Paul Volcker's campaigns in the early 1980s.
Conrad knows what aggressive tightening costs. He has done it before, in 1980, and it worked and it broke things and the things it broke were mostly Simón's. He is doing it again for the same reason — inflation is real and must be addressed — and he knows exactly what it will cost Simón, and he is doing it anyway, because the alternative is worse for everyone including Simón, and this is the logic of the reserve currency: the decisions that are right for the system are not always right for every part of the system.
A press conference — the particular architecture of the Federal Reserve announcement: the podium, the microphones, the room of journalists with their calculators already running. Conrad is behind the podium as the decision is read. Aurora is watching from somewhere and her price is already registering it. Simón is on the phone with his finance minister. Hiro is watching the yen move on a screen in Tokyo and the number is going the wrong direction.
SIMÓN (to Conrad, on a call that Conrad takes at the end of a long day): "You know what this does to our debt service." CONRAD: "Yes." SIMÓN: "You're doing it anyway." CONRAD: "Yes." SIMÓN: "I want that on the record." CONRAD: "It's always on the record, Simón. That's what I am. I'm the record."
#224 · 2022 Zero Too Long
Hiro Nakamura, Conrad Powers
The Japanese yen falls to its weakest level against the dollar in thirty-two years in 2022 — briefly touching 150 yen per dollar — as the Bank of Japan maintains its ultra-loose monetary policy while the Federal Reserve aggressively raises rates.
Hiro is meticulous, patient, and has a longer memory than almost anyone in the room. He held rates at zero because Japan's deflation was real and the wounds from 1990 were still healing and it was the right decision for a long time. The problem with decisions that are right for a long time is that the world keeps moving and eventually the right decision becomes a cage. The Fed is raising while Hiro holds, and the gap between them is showing up in his face — in the exchange rate, which is the most public measure of what the market thinks of you.
A trading floor in Tokyo at night — the Tokyo session's particular blue light, the screens with the yen quote, the line moving in one direction with the patience of gravity. Hiro at a desk separate from the floor, reviewing the charts. He has seen worse than this in terms of damage. He has not seen this particular kind of helplessness — the helplessness of being right about your own situation and wrong about the world's situation and watching the world's situation win.
HIRO (to himself, to the chart, to the thirty-two-year record appearing on his screen): "I was not wrong to hold. The argument was correct. The world just decided to run a different argument — faster, louder, more expensive." A beat. "I will hold until I cannot. And then I will move. And when I move it will be deliberate." He pauses. "It is always deliberate."
#225 · 2022 Not Destroyed
Marisol Vega, Aurora Lux, Conrad Powers
The crypto exchange FTX collapses in November 2022, erasing roughly $8 billion in customer funds and leading to the arrest of its founder Sam Bankman-Fried; Bitcoin falls below $17,000 from its $69,000 peak a year earlier.
Marisol makes a distinction that she will make again: discredited is not destroyed. The structures that survive crises are not the ones that avoid failure — they are the ones that survive their own failure and adapt. The stranger from 2009 has just had his most public crisis, and the coverage is treating it as a verdict. Marisol does not treat it as a verdict. She treats it as data. The underlying technology is not FTX. The idea is not the exchange.
The aftermath room — not the event itself but the morning after. The printed headlines on someone's desk. The graphs showing the price collapse. A congressional hearing scheduled. Lawyers arriving. Marisol is not in any of these rooms. She is in a quiet room with her instruments, writing a note titled 'The Distinction.' Aurora stops by. She looks at the note without reading it. She looks at Marisol.
AURORA (to Marisol): "You're not writing an obituary." MARISOL: "No." AURORA: "What are you writing?" MARISOL: "A distinction. Between the thing that failed and the idea underneath it." She pauses. "Every institution I've watched get built started with an idea that outlived its first failure." AURORA (quietly): "I know. I've watched all of them."
#226 · 2022 The Domestic Wound
Wei Lin, Mei Lin
Chinese property developer Evergrande defaults on its international debt obligations in 2021, setting off a prolonged crisis in China's property sector that wipes out the savings of millions of homebuyers and strains the financial system through 2023.
Wei Lin has built. That is what he does — he accumulates, he constructs, he extends. The property sector was both the vehicle and the symbol: Chinese families putting their savings into homes not yet built, trusting that the construction would come. When Evergrande falls it is not just a company. It is the promise of a particular kind of ascent — the apartment as the measure of arrival — and the people holding the undelivered keys are not institutions. They are families.
A construction site in a Chinese city — the crane that has not moved in months, the half-finished towers with no workers, the padlocked gate. Families gathered outside with documents, their contracts, their deposit receipts, their photographs of the architectural renderings they were shown when they paid. Mei Lin stands at a distance from Wei Lin, watching the same scene. They rarely look at each other directly.
MEI LIN (to Wei Lin, both looking at the locked gate): "This is what Conrad had in 2008." WEI LIN: "It is not the same." MEI LIN: "The families standing outside with contracts they cannot redeem — that part is the same." WEI LIN (a long pause): "Yes." He does not say more. That is the acknowledgment.
#227 · 2022 A New Exchange
Hiro Nakamura, Marisol Vega, Conrad Powers
OpenAI launches ChatGPT on November 30, 2022; it reaches one million users in five days and becomes the fastest-growing consumer application in history, triggering a global race in artificial intelligence.
Hiro sees it first. He has always had a particular sensitivity to inflection points — the moments when the direction of a thing changes — because he lives inside one of the longest, most patient economies on earth, one that has been waiting for its next inflection for thirty years. ChatGPT is not the thing itself. It is the first public face of the thing. But Hiro has been watching the underlying research and he knows what follows a public face: acceleration, then disruption, then rearrangement of who does what for whom and at what price.
A laptop screen displaying the ChatGPT interface — the clean white box, the blinking cursor, the first response appearing word by word. Hiro is sitting with it at an otherwise empty table. He types a question about monetary policy and reads the answer with total attention. Then he types another question, this one about labor displacement. Then he closes the laptop and sits for a long time.
HIRO (to Marisol, on the call later that night): "This is not a search engine. It is not a database. It is something that exchanges — knowledge, analysis, synthesis — at a speed and scale no human institution has achieved." A pause. "It is an Exchange. A new kind. And like every new Exchange, we have no idea yet who benefits and who does not." Another pause. "I find this genuinely exciting. I thought you should know."
#228 · 2023 She Does Not Leave
Aurora Lux, Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega
Gold prices cross $2,000 per ounce again in early 2023 and hold above that level for the remainder of the year, with central banks worldwide purchasing gold at the highest annual rate since 1967.
Aurora has been above $2,000 before — in 2020, briefly, in 2022, briefly. Both times the market found a reason to pull her back. This time she crosses $2,000 and stays. There is a difference in her bearing — not triumph, nothing so simple — but something settled. She has been here before and left. Now she has been here before and stayed. The staying is the new information. Conrad watches. He knows what gold above $2,000 means as a sustained signal. It means someone is hedging against something.
A long, quiet room with a Reuters terminal displaying gold price history — the chart of Aurora's entire modern existence, from Bretton Woods at $35, to Nixon's break at $42, through the '80 peak and the '99 low and the 2008 surge and the 2011 high and the 2020 cross and now the steady, settled line above $2,000 that does not dip back. Aurora in the room with Marisol, who is annotating the chart. Conrad visible in the doorway but not entering.
AURORA (studying her own chart, the line that holds): "Every other time I came here, I left." MARISOL: "What's different now?" AURORA (a long pause, her finger tracing the sustained line): "Every other time, they believed the emergency was over. Now they're not sure the emergency is over." She steps back from the chart. "I stay when the fear has no name."
#229 · 2023 New Guest List
Wei Lin, Simón Reyes, Zane Mercer, Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega
At the BRICS summit in Johannesburg in August 2023, the bloc invites Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Egypt, Iran, Ethiopia, and Argentina to join, nearly doubling its membership in the most significant expansion since the group's founding.
Conrad has attended the right tables for his entire existence — the G7, the IMF board, the SWIFT governors. He has set the guest lists. The BRICS expansion is not a rival system yet. It is not a threat in the immediate, operational sense. But it is the first time in his life that a significant number of the world's nations have gathered at a table he did not set, and the guest list includes countries that produce a majority of the world's oil, food, and rare earths.
A summit hall in Johannesburg — the flags of the new members being arranged beside the original five. Press cameras. Handshakes. The formal photographs that will be studied. Wei Lin moves through the room with the ease of someone who has been planning this for years. Simón is present with a measured expression that says he understands the significance and is not ready to fully commit to it but is also not leaving. Zane is everywhere and nowhere — he is represented at this table by several delegations simultaneously.
MARISOL (to Conrad, reviewing the guest list): "Saudi Arabia. The UAE. Iran. Egypt. Ethiopia." CONRAD: "I see it." MARISOL: "That's most of the world's energy production." CONRAD: "I see it." MARISOL: "You need to stop saying 'I see it' and tell me what you're going to do about it." CONRAD (a long pause): "I'm going to watch. Which I have not always done first. Watching first is new."
#230 · 2023 The Social Media Bank Run
Marisol Vega, Conrad Powers
Silicon Valley Bank collapses on March 10, 2023 — the largest U.S. bank failure since 2008 — after a bank run accelerated by social media posts and instant digital withdrawals that drained $42 billion in deposits in a single day.
Marisol flagged this. She flagged the concentration of uninsured deposits, the duration mismatch in the bond portfolio, the sector concentration. She flagged it in reports that went up the chain and came back annotated with reassurances. The thing she did not flag — because she had not yet measured it — was the speed. Bank runs in 1930 required physical presence: bodies in lines, the visible queue, the ten-minute delay between decision and action. This run happened inside phones, in minutes.
The SVB lobby in Santa Clara — the kind of corporate lobby that was designed to communicate stability: marble, light wood, contemporary art, the smell of a place that expected to be permanent. The empty lobby after the FDIC takes over. Marisol walks through it not for forensics but for the feeling — the specific feeling of institutional confidence in the afternoon and institutional failure by morning. She is updating her models.
MARISOL (in the lobby, to Conrad on the phone): "The bank run took eleven hours. 1930 would have taken three weeks." CONRAD: "The system worked. FDIC stepped in." MARISOL: "The system worked this time. What I'm telling you is that the physics of failure has changed. The speed has changed. And your interventions were designed for the old physics." A pause. "I'm updating the models."
#231 · 2023 The Veto
Conrad Powers, Simón Reyes, Marisol Vega
The Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023 and the subsequent Israeli military campaign in Gaza kill over 40,000 Palestinians by the end of 2024; the United States vetoes multiple ceasefire resolutions in the UN Security Council.
Conrad's credibility is not only financial. It has never been only financial. It is also moral — or has presented itself as moral — and the veto is a moral act that the Global South reads differently than Conrad reads it. Simón has learned to separate Conrad-the-currency from Conrad-the-foreign-policy and has mostly kept those ledgers separate. This moment makes the ledgers harder to separate. What Marisol is watching is not the conflict itself but the way the conflict is changing the language people use around dedollarization — the way it moves from economic argument to moral argument.
A UN Security Council chamber — the round table, the name placards, the simultaneous interpreters in their glass booths. The vote on the ceasefire resolution. Conrad's representative raises the hand that signals veto. Simón watches from his seat. He does not raise his voice. He does not look away. He writes something on his notepad, tears it out, folds it, puts it in his pocket.
SIMÓN (to Marisol, in the corridor after the vote): "He will tell me this is complicated. That there is context. That the situation is not simple." MARISOL: "Is he wrong?" SIMÓN: "He is not wrong. He is also not what he said he was." A beat. "Those can both be true. I have learned to hold two true things."
#232 · 2023 The Question on Every Cover
Aurora Lux, Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega, Wei Lin
2023 sees an unprecedented wave of mainstream media coverage questioning dollar dominance, with major publications including The Economist, Foreign Affairs, and the Wall Street Journal running cover stories on dedollarization.
Aurora is asked in every interview. She has developed an answer that is not an answer — a form of response that contains the question back inside itself, that gives the interviewer the sensation of a reply without giving them a position she does not want to take publicly. She does this not out of evasion but out of accuracy: she genuinely does not know the answer, and she has been around long enough to know that certainty on this question is always premature.
An interview setting — the lights, the chairs at forty-five degrees, the small microphone clipped to Aurora's collar. The interviewer with their printed questions. Aurora in her composed, attentive posture that she has had since before the interviewer was born. The question comes: Is the dollar losing its dominance?
AURORA (to the interviewer, to the camera, to the record): "The dollar has survived everything since 1944. He has survived Bretton Woods and its end, the oil shock, the Volcker recession, 2008, COVID. He is extraordinary." A pause. She adjusts nothing. "What I can tell you is that gold does not move the way it has moved in the last three years because nothing is changing. It moves because something is changing. I don't tell you what. I show you."
#233 · 2024 The Second Time
Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega, Sofia Valente, Wei Lin, Simón Reyes
Donald Trump wins the 2024 U.S. presidential election, becoming the first president in American history to be elected to non-consecutive terms.
The first time, everyone recalibrated. The second time, those who recalibrated the first time do not have to recalibrate again — they already hold the updated position. What Marisol notes is that the second election moves faster. The diplomatic calls happen within hours, not days. The currency market adjustments are pre-positioned. The world has practiced for this. The world knowing how to practice for this is, itself, a kind of answer to a question about Conrad's uniqueness that nobody is asking aloud.
An election night set — the map filling in, the states turning red or blue, the coverage that goes until 2 a.m. Conrad is watching from a room he has watched from before. The sensation is recursive: the second time feels both familiar and strange, the way a scar feels familiar and strange. Marisol is at her instruments. They are moving, but the movements are orderly. The market has already priced most of this.
MARISOL (to Conrad, as the result becomes clear): "The first time, the world was surprised. This time they were ready." CONRAD: "Is that better or worse?" MARISOL: "It's different. 'Ready' means they have made arrangements. 'Ready' means they are not waiting for you to be someone you are not." She pauses. "Whether that's better or worse depends on how much you needed them to be waiting."
#234 · 2024 Someone Is Hedging
Aurora Lux, Marisol Vega, Wei Lin, Mei Lin, Charlotte Ashworth, Conrad Powers
Central banks worldwide purchase a net 1,037 tonnes of gold in 2024, the third consecutive year of purchases exceeding 1,000 tonnes — the highest sustained buying rate since the end of the Bretton Woods system; China, India, and several European nations are among the largest buyers.
Aurora notices when she is being purchased. She has always known who is in the room — who is buying quietly, who is selling, who is hedging, who is declaring. What is unusual in 2024 is not any single buyer but the breadth: Wei Lin buying, yes — she has been watching Wei Lin accumulate for a decade. But Charlotte, too. And the Gulf states. And several central banks that have never meaningfully held gold before. When everyone is buying and no one is announcing, it means something specific.
Aurora's room — the vault-adjacent space she occupies in her own mind, where she keeps track of who holds what. She is updating a ledger that exists nowhere else. The additions to the ledger are coming faster than at any point since 1971. Marisol arrives. Aurora shows her the ledger without comment. Marisol reads it. She looks up.
MARISOL (reading the ledger, looking up): "Charlotte is buying?" AURORA: "Quietly." MARISOL: "She held below five percent reserves in gold for years." AURORA: "She is holding more now." MARISOL: "Someone is hedging." AURORA: "Everyone is hedging. The interesting question is what they think they are hedging against." A pause. "I don't answer that question. I am the answer to that question."
#235 · 2024 The Productivity Argument
Hiro Nakamura, Marisol Vega, Conrad Powers
AI adoption accelerates dramatically in 2024 as major corporations deploy large language models across knowledge-work functions; economists debate whether the productivity gains will mirror the 1990s technology boom or represent a structural change of greater magnitude.
Hiro has been right about this for two years and he finds being right about things he finds important to be one of the few genuine pleasures remaining to him. He is careful not to perform this pleasure. He is careful to frame his correctness as a question still being answered rather than an answer already given. What he is actually worried about is the distribution: in the 1990s, the productivity gains went somewhere — not evenly, but somewhere. The AI productivity gains are going somewhere too. He is watching the somewhere carefully.
An economics conference room — the kind with a whiteboard and printed slide decks and economists in business casual arguing in the specific way economists argue, which is with measured voices and very strong opinions. Hiro is at the table. He is not arguing. He is listening with the patience of someone who has a longer timeline than everyone else in the room and knows it.
HIRO (finally, when asked directly, to the room): "The 1990s productivity argument was correct. The distribution of that productivity was a separate question. We celebrated the argument and forgot the question." He looks at the slides. "I'd suggest we not make the same sequence of errors. The argument about AI productivity is probably correct. The question about its distribution is just beginning." A pause. "The question is always the interesting part."
#236 · 2025 The Shudder
Conrad Powers, Simón Reyes, Sofia Valente, Wei Lin
The United States imposes sweeping tariffs on imports from most trading partners in early 2025, with rates reaching 145% on Chinese goods; global supply chains restructure and trade volumes contract as countries redirect exports and seek new partners.
The post-1945 trade architecture was Conrad's design — not his alone, but built on his currency, settled in his system. He is now imposing tariffs that challenge the architecture he built. Simón's export markets are drying up because Conrad has decided to change the rules of a game he invented. Simón has seen Conrad change rules before — 1971, 1973, the Brady Bonds, the SWIFT sanctions — but those changes were responses to crises. This change is a choice. The distinction matters. Choices can be made again. In different directions.
A shipping container port — containers stacked in their geometry of primary colors, the cranes in their long silhouettes. The movement is slower than it was six months ago. The booking rates are down. Some lanes are empty. The port worker who has the same shift he has had for fifteen years has a different kind of business this quarter. Simón walks the dock without looking like he's walking the dock. He is counting what is not here.
SIMÓN (to Conrad, on a call that Conrad takes in an office somewhere, distracted): "You built this system. The rules of trade. The settlement currencies. The institutions." CONRAD: "I'm adjusting them." SIMÓN: "You're breaking them." CONRAD: "I'm protecting what's mine." SIMÓN (quietly): "What you called 'the system' used to be the protection. Now the protection is against the system." A beat. "I'm writing that down."
#237 · 2025 Three Thousand
Aurora Lux, Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega
Gold crosses $3,000 per ounce for the first time in February 2025, driven by central bank purchases, geopolitical uncertainty, inflation concerns, and dollar credibility questions; it continues rising through the spring.
Aurora has thought about what she would say at $3,000. She has had time to think about it because the number has been approaching for years and she is old enough to know when a number is approaching. She has decided not to say the thing everyone expects — the triumphant reading of her own price, the positioning. She will say something more precise. Because $3,000 is not triumph. $3,000 is information. The information is that a very large number of very serious institutions have decided that holding her is more important than whatever they were holding before her.
A trading screen — the number $3,000.00 on the screen with the small green arrow beside it. Someone has taken a photograph of the screen. The photograph is circulating. Journalists are calling Aurora for comment. Conrad has not called. Aurora is in her own room with the number and she is not looking at it with pride. She is looking at it the way a doctor looks at a vital sign that is very high — with attention, with care, with the understanding that extremity is always information.
AURORA (to Marisol, who has come to ask what she thinks): "They are asking me if I'm celebrating." MARISOL: "Are you?" AURORA: "I am at three thousand dollars an ounce because trust is leaving somewhere else. I do not celebrate the thing that sends people to me. I receive them." A pause. "When gold rises this fast, something is afraid. I don't know yet what it is. But I know what I am. I am where the afraid go."
#238 · 2025 The Alarm
Marisol Vega, Conrad Powers, Aurora Lux
U.S. Treasury markets experience unusual volatility in 2025, with rapid swings in long-term yields unsettling investors; analysts point to growing concerns about U.S. fiscal sustainability, with the national debt exceeding $36 trillion.
Marisol's instruments are designed to detect stress in the system. They were not designed to detect stress in Conrad specifically — he was supposed to be the instrument, not the subject. But the Treasury market is Conrad's debt and the volatility in Conrad's debt is the instrument alarming. She does not tell Conrad this in the way you tell someone their house is on fire. She tells him in the way you tell a doctor that the patient is his own chart. He has to understand that the thing that is stressed is not external to him. It is him. He is the bond that is volatile.
Marisol's monitoring space — the screens, the feeds, the yield curve displayed across multiple monitors showing the unusual shape, the volatility bands wider than they have been in decades. She is not panicking. Panicking is not a tool she has. She is annotating. She is building the report. Conrad arrives and she turns the screen toward him and he looks at his own yield curve — his own debt — moving with the restlessness of a market that is not sure of something it used to be sure of.
MARISOL (turning the monitor to face him): "This is your debt. Not your opponent's debt. Not Simón's. Yours." CONRAD (looking at it steadily): "I know what a yield curve is, Marisol." MARISOL: "Then you know what it means when it moves like this." CONRAD (a long pause, looking at his own volatility): "It means someone isn't sure." MARISOL: "It means the someone includes people who are usually very sure."
#239 · 2025 Below Fifty
Conrad Powers, Wei Lin, Mei Lin, Simón Reyes, Marisol Vega
By 2025, the U.S. dollar's share of global trade settlements falls below 50% for the first time in the modern era, as yuan, rupee, dirham, and other currency settlement networks expand; the dollar retains majority of foreign exchange reserves but its trade settlement dominance has structurally shifted.
Conrad has heard the dedollarization argument for twenty years and has been right to dismiss it in its most dramatic forms. He is still the reserve currency. He still settles the majority of global financial transactions. But the trade settlement number — below fifty percent — is a threshold, and thresholds matter even when they are arbitrary, because they change the story people tell, and stories about money are also money. Marisol has been waiting for this number.
A room with a data terminal showing the settlement currency breakdown — the pie chart that Conrad has led for the entirety of his modern existence, the slice that was always largest and is now, for the first time, less than half. Wei Lin's slice is visible. The rupee's slice. The dirham's. Conrad standing in front of the chart. Not moving.
MARISOL (beside him, looking at the chart): "It's a threshold. Not a verdict." CONRAD: "I know the difference." MARISOL: "I know you do. I'm saying it anyway because I want it on record that I said it to you directly and that you heard me." CONRAD (still looking at the chart): "What else is on record?" MARISOL: "That you've been here before. 1945. You were new. You were building. You didn't know you would win. And you did the work anyway." She pauses. "The work is the part that's still available to you."
#240 · 2025 The Canary
Aurora Lux, Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega, Hiro Nakamura
Gold reaches $3,200 per ounce in spring 2025 and continues rising, with analysts projecting further gains; central bank buying accelerates and retail investment inflows set records.
Aurora at $3,200 is not the same as Aurora at $3,000. At $3,000, she could be called a milestone, a round number, a psychological threshold. At $3,200 and continuing, she is a sustained signal. She is where the money goes when the money does not know where else to go. She does not want this responsibility — or she has wanted it for so long that wanting and not wanting have collapsed into the same thing. What she feels most precisely is the weight of being correct without having been able to say what was coming.
A financial news broadcast — the crawl at the bottom of the screen with the gold price updating in real time. Behind the anchor, a large graphic: GOLD: $3,247.80. Aurora is watching from somewhere that is not a television set. She is watching the way the number moves. Slowly, deliberately, with the patience of something very old that has decided to go somewhere and is going there.
AURORA (to Conrad, who has finally called): "You want to know what it means." CONRAD: "Yes." AURORA: "It means the trust is leaving somewhere. Not arriving here — leaving there. I am not the destination. I am the indicator." CONRAD: "Where is it leaving?" AURORA (quietly): "You know where. You have always known where. The question you're really asking is whether it's coming back." She pauses. "That is a question only you can answer."
#241 · 2025 Regulate the Algorithm
Marisol Vega, Hiro Nakamura, Sofia Valente
The European Union implements the AI Act in 2025, the world's first comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence regulation, establishing risk tiers and compliance requirements for AI systems.
Sofia has always been the one who writes the rules — who believes that the architecture of governance is as important as the force it governs. She approaches AI regulation the way she approached the Euro: with the conviction that if you build the institution carefully, the behavior will follow. Hiro watches her draft the regulations with the patience of someone who has seen many regulatory frameworks and knows their limits. Marisol annotates the draft in the margins. Her annotations are mostly questions.
A drafting room in Brussels — the particular fluorescence of regulatory work, the multiple printouts of successive drafts, the coffee cups, the lawyers and technical experts in the same room trying to speak the same language. Sofia moves through the room with authority. She knows this room. She has built things in rooms like this before.
SOFIA (to Hiro, holding the latest draft): "If we don't write the rules, someone else does." HIRO: "Someone is already writing them. The companies writing the systems are also writing the rules embedded in those systems." SOFIA: "That is why we need these." She holds up the document. HIRO (gently): "Yes. I agree. I only want to make sure we know what we are regulating — and that we know that what we are regulating will have changed before the regulation takes effect." A pause. "It always has."
#242 · 2025 Fifty Years After the Gold Window
Aurora Lux, Conrad Powers
2025 marks the 54th anniversary of the Nixon Shock — the closing of the gold window in August 1971 — and prompts extensive commentary on what the post-Bretton Woods era has produced and what comes next.
Conrad and Aurora are in the same room for the anniversary conversation and they both know the conversation has never been finished. He divorced her — that is how she experienced 1971 — and he has managed extraordinarily well since the divorce, by most measures, for most of the people inside his system. She has also done well since the divorce, by her measures. But the conversation about what they are to each other now — not anchored, not fixed, not $35 or $42 or any fixed rate, but still present, still relevant, still in the same room — that conversation has been deferred for fifty-four years and at $3,200 it can no longer be deferred.
The same room from #001 and #074 — or a room that feels like it. Something with weight. Something that has held important conversations before. The two of them. No one else. The gold price visible somewhere — a screen, a number on a phone — not the subject of the conversation but present in the room the way a vital sign is present.
AURORA (to Conrad, finally): "You left me in 1971. You've done very well since." CONRAD: "Yes." AURORA: "And I've done very well since." CONRAD: "Yes." A long pause. "Are we having the conversation now?" AURORA: "I think the market is having it for us. I think the market has been having it for three years and we've been letting it." She meets his eyes. "I'd rather we have it ourselves."
#243 · 2025 The BRICS Currency Proposal
Wei Lin, Simón Reyes, Zane Mercer, Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega
BRICS nations advance discussions in 2025 about a common settlement unit or reserve asset, while stopping short of a formal currency launch; the proposal generates significant press coverage but founders on the fundamental divergence of member interests.
Marisol has been waiting for this proposal to collide with its own contradictions. A BRICS currency would require Wei Lin to share monetary sovereignty with Simón, and Zane's producers, and India, who is not in the room but is watching from it. These are countries with different inflation rates, different debt levels, different political systems, different priorities. What binds them is the desire to not be in Conrad's position. That is a real desire. It is not, however, a monetary system.
A summit negotiating room — the private session after the photo opportunity, where the proposal lands on the actual technicalities. The delegations have been talking in generalities. The technicalities are specific and the specifics are where the proposal begins to lose its shape. Wei Lin is patient. He knows what he is and is not willing to give up. Simón has a list. The list and Wei Lin's positions do not overlap as much as the press release implied.
MARISOL (to Conrad, reading the summit communiqué): "It says 'exploring alternative settlement mechanisms.'" CONRAD: "That's not a currency." MARISOL: "No. But it was a real conversation and it will be a real conversation again, with better architecture, when the pain of staying in your system exceeds the pain of building a new one." She puts down the communiqué. "They're not there yet. But the distance is shorter than it was."
#244 · 2025 The Ceiling Again
Conrad Powers, Marisol Vega
The United States reaches its statutory debt ceiling in 2025, triggering negotiations that temporarily halt certain government payments and produce ratings agency warnings; the ceiling is ultimately raised after weeks of political confrontation.
Conrad has done this before. He has been through debt ceiling crises in 2011 and 2013 and 2023 and he is through this one too. The crisis resolves. The ceiling is raised. The dollar survives. What Marisol is watching is not the crisis itself but the international audience watching the crisis — the central banks, the foreign governments, the sovereign wealth funds that hold $3 trillion of U.S. Treasuries and watch America's political system threaten to default on them as a negotiating tactic.
A cable news set — the countdown clock that appears when a deadline is approaching, the political commentators in split screen, the urgency that is performed and the urgency that is real interleaved. Marisol is not watching the news set. She is watching the foreign exchange flows and the Treasury auction results, which tell a different story from the cable news in the specific way that markets tell different stories from commentary.
MARISOL (to Conrad, while watching the auction results): "You know what's interesting? The auction cleared. Foreign buyers showed up." CONRAD: "Good." MARISOL: "They showed up and they demanded a higher yield than last quarter." CONRAD: "That's the market." MARISOL: "That's the market pricing in the possibility that next time you might not resolve it." She pauses. "They still believe you. They're charging you for the uncertainty of that belief. That is new."
#245 · 2025 Simón's Restructuring
Simón Reyes, Conrad Powers, Wei Lin
Multiple emerging market economies undertake formal debt restructurings in 2023–2025, with Zambia, Ghana, Sri Lanka, and others completing IMF-backed programs as the high interest rate environment following COVID relief borrowing creates unsustainable debt loads.
Simón has restructured before. He has always restructured with Conrad's institutions — the IMF, the Paris Club, the framework of rules that Conrad built and that Simón has never fully owned. This restructuring is different in one way: Wei Lin is at the table for some of it, as a bilateral creditor who has lent directly and must now negotiate terms. The creditor table has more chairs than it used to. The negotiation is more complicated. The resolution takes longer.
A negotiating hotel in some capital city that is not the debtor country — Geneva, Paris, Washington, the international geography of debt management. Simón at the table. Conrad's institutions on one side. Wei Lin's representatives on the other. Neither is willing to move first on haircuts. Simón is watching both and calculating and he is very tired in a way that shows in nothing except the particular stillness of his hands.
SIMÓN (to both sides, at the end of a long day): "You are both my creditors. You are not coordinating with each other. I am coordinating between you." A pause. "I would like to note that this is not a service I agreed to provide when I borrowed the money. But I'm providing it. Because someone has to." He stands. "I'll see you both tomorrow."
#246 · 2025 Hiro Moves
Hiro Nakamura, Conrad Powers, Aurora Lux
The Bank of Japan raises interest rates in 2024 and 2025, ending its era of ultra-loose monetary policy; the moves cause significant global market volatility as the yen carry trade — in which investors borrowed cheaply in yen to buy higher-yielding assets worldwide — unwinds.
Hiro has said he would be deliberate. He is deliberate. The rate normalization is not a surprise — he has been telegraphing it for months, in the careful language of a central bank that does not want to move markets precipitously but must move. What is a surprise is the scale of the global reaction — the carry trade unwind, the volatility in assets far from Japan that had been financed by Hiro's cheap yen for years without anyone calling it what it was: a global subsidy from Hiro's low rates to the rest of the world's risk appetite.
A Bank of Japan board meeting — the formality of it, the translated minutes that will be parsed by traders in every time zone. Hiro at the head of the table or near it, delivering the decision in the measured cadences of institutional Japanese. Then the markets: screens lighting up, positions moving, the global discovery of interconnection. Aurora watches from somewhere and thinks: this is what it looks like when patience ends. Not violence. Deliberateness.
HIRO (to Conrad, after the global reaction): "I told you I would move when I was ready." CONRAD: "The market didn't expect the scale of the reaction." HIRO: "The market borrowed from me for fifteen years and called it their own strategy. When I changed my terms, they discovered it was my strategy all along." A pause, something in him that is not quite satisfaction but is adjacent to it. "I have been very patient. I am still being patient. Patience does not mean unchanged forever."
#247 · 2025 Charlotte's Deals
Charlotte Ashworth, Conrad Powers, Sofia Valente
Britain continues negotiating post-Brexit trade agreements in 2024–2025, reaching accords with Gulf states and advancing talks with India, while also beginning to re-examine aspects of its relationship with the EU under a new Labour government.
Charlotte does not ask for help. She has not asked for help since the 1940s and she will not start now. She is managing the wound she gave herself with Brexit and she is managing it with the particular dignity of someone who made the choice, understood the cost, and is paying the cost without complaint even when the cost is higher than she estimated. The new trade deals are real. They are smaller than what she had before. She is building something. It is not what she had. She is building it anyway.
A trade ministry room — the working version, not the ceremonial: papers, legal texts, position documents, the back-and-forth of negotiators who have been in the same building for six months. Charlotte in the room as she always has been — composed, specific, knowing what she wants and what she will accept and what she will not accept and keeping those three lists current and separate.
CHARLOTTE (to Sofia, who has found a moment to check in): "You want to ask if I regret it." SOFIA: "I know better than to ask." CHARLOTTE: "Then what are you asking?" SOFIA (carefully): "I'm asking if you need anything." Charlotte looks at her for a long moment. "I need the next deal to close. I need the one after that." She looks back at her documents. "What I need is work. Which I have." A pause. "Thank you for asking."
#248 · 2025 Sofia and Wei Lin: A Chapter That Goes Nowhere
Sofia Valente, Wei Lin, Conrad Powers
EU-China relations experience a pattern of engagement and retreat in 2024–2025, with trade tensions, EV tariffs, and investment screening complicating diplomatic efforts; a planned summit produces limited outcomes.
Sofia and Wei Lin have been circling a potential partnership for years that neither can fully commit to — she cannot give him what he wants (market access without reciprocal rules), he cannot give her what she wants (openness without strategic control). They meet with the politeness of two people who understand each other well enough to know they are not going to agree and who are not ready to stop trying. Conrad watches from a distance with the particular attention of a dominant power watching two significant entities discover the limits of their relationship.
A summit room in Brussels or Beijing — the formal setting that implies more progress than is occurring. The agenda is long. The outcomes are short. Sofia and Wei Lin have the dinner that takes place after the formal sessions, where they speak more directly, and what they say to each other in the dinner room is more interesting than the communiqué.
WEI LIN (at dinner, after the communiqué language has been agreed): "You know this goes nowhere today." SOFIA: "I know." WEI LIN: "Then why are we here?" SOFIA: "Because the conversation that goes nowhere today is the foundation of the conversation that goes somewhere in five years. I am a patient builder, Wei Lin. I thought you were too." WEI LIN (looking at her): "I am." A pause. "Five years." SOFIA: "If we're lucky."
#249 · 2025 The End of the Era
Conrad Powers, Aurora Lux, Marisol Vega, Wei Lin, Simón Reyes, Hiro Nakamura, Sofia Valente, Charlotte Ashworth, Zane Mercer, Mei Lin
As 2025 ends, the United States approaches the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence in a world more multipolar, more contested, more technologically disrupted, and more uncertain than at any point in Conrad's existence.
The era does not end with a crisis or a resolution. It ends with an inventory. Every character in the room has changed — not fundamentally, because they cannot change fundamentally; they are what they are. But they have been tested by this era in ways that have revealed what they are made of and what they are not made of. Conrad is still the system. He is not sure what that means the way he was once sure. Aurora is at $3,200 and climbing and she holds the question everyone is too afraid to ask directly.
A year-end room — the kind that exists in the space between Christmas and New Year's, when the markets are thin and the offices are quiet and the people who make decisions are in other rooms and the instruments are still running because instruments don't close for the holiday. Every character is somewhere in this room in their own way. The number on the screen is $3,200 gold and $36 trillion debt and below-50% dollar trade settlement.
MARISOL (to the room, to whoever is listening, to the instruments, to herself): "The question going into the 250th year is not whether Conrad survives. He survives. He has always survived. The question is what he survives as." She pauses. "That question has never been asked before, not like this, not by this many people at the same time." She looks at the instruments. "The 250th year will answer it. Or it will defer it. Those are the only two options. And I have learned, in my time watching all of this, that deferral is also a kind of answer."
Era 10 — The 250th Year
2026
1 moment
#250 · 2026 Still Here
Conrad Powers, Aurora Lux, Charlotte Windsor, Sofia Valente, Hiro Nakamura, Zane Mercer, Wei Lin, Mei Lin, Simón Reyes, Marisol Vega
On July 4, 2026, the United States marks the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence — the semiquincentennial — in a world more contested, more multipolar, and more uncertain than at any point in the republic's history, as gold trades above $3,000 per ounce and the architecture of global finance continues its slow, structural shift.
Conrad has survived everything. He knows this. The knowing is not comfort — it is weight. He has survived because he adapted, because he was useful, because he was dominant, because he was feared, because he was trusted, because he was weaponized, because he was the only option, because he became the least bad option, because he was needed even by the people who needed him less than before. He has arrived at 250 carrying every version of himself he has ever been: the revolutionary scrip that wasn't trusted, the gold-backed promise that lasted twenty-six years, the reserve currency that was supposed to be temporary, the weapon, the safe haven, the instrument of sanction, the contested medium. All of them. He has not shed any of them. They are all here in the room.
Independence Hall in Philadelphia — the original room, or a room that holds the original room inside it. July heat pressing through the tall windows. The smell of old wood and stone, the smell of a building that was built to last and has lasted and shows the cost of lasting. The table is long and every character is at it — the first time in 250 moments that all of them are in the same room at the same time. Outside, the city celebrating, the fireworks not yet, just the afternoon and the heat and the particular American July that has always been both festivity and reckoning.
AURORA (to Conrad, across the table, to the room, to the record, in a voice that is neither triumph nor despair but something more durable than either): "You are still here." A pause — the length of a room full of history. "So am I." She holds his gaze. "The question was never whether you would last 250 years. Things that are necessary tend to last." Her hand flat on the table, not reaching for his, present in the room. "The question was always what you would become. What you would choose to be, when you had the power to choose. What you would protect and what you would sacrifice and what you would admit you got wrong." Outside, the first sounds of the celebration beginning. "Two hundred and fifty years." She looks at him — the whole look, the five-thousand-year look. "You have done things I could not forgive and things I could not have done without you." The room holds this. "I don't know what comes next. I have never known what comes next. I have only ever known what holds." Her hand, still flat on the table, the gold of her pressed against the old wood of the original room. "The question for the next 250 years is the same question as the first. It has always been the same question." She says it quietly: "Will you be worth trusting?"
